analogy: Think of "Magnetism and Matter" through the chapter's own opening images: Physics 136 5.1 INTRODUCTION Magnetic phenomena are universal in nature. Vast, distant galaxies, the tiny invisible atoms, humans and beasts all are permeated through and through with a host of magnetic fields from a variety of sources. The earth’s magnetism predates human evolution. The word... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 5.1 INTRODUCTION, MAGNETISM AND, MATTER, FIGURE FIGURE FIGURE FIGURE FIGURE 5.1 5.1 5.1 5.1 5.1 The arrangement. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Magnetism and Matter is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that physics 136 5.1 INTRODUCTION Magnetic phenomena are universal in nature.

The text explains that vast, distant galaxies, the tiny invisible atoms, humans and beasts all are permeated through and through with a host of magnetic fields from a variety of sources.

The text explains that the earth’s magnetism predates human evolution.

The text explains that the word magnet is derived from the name of an island in Greece called magnesia where magnetic ore deposits were found, as early as 600 BC.

The text explains that in the previous chapter we have learned that moving charges or electric currents produce magnetic fields.

The text explains that this discovery, which was made in the early part of the nineteenth century is credited to Oersted, Ampere, Biot and Savart, among others.

The text explains that in the present chapter, we take a look at magnetism as a subject in its own right.

The text explains that some of the commonly known ideas regarding magnetism are: (i) The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south to the north.

The text explains that (ii) When a bar magnet is freely suspended, it points in the north-south direction.

The text explains that the tip which points to the geographic north is called the north pole and the tip which points to the geographic south is called the south pole of the magnet.

The text explains that 137 Magnetism and Matter (iii) There is a repulsive force when north poles ( or south poles ) of two magnets are brought close together.

The text explains that conversely, there is an attractive force between the north pole of one magnet and the south pole of the other.

The text explains that (iv) We cannot isolate the north, or south pole of a magnet.

The text explains that if a bar magnet is broken into two halves, we get two similar bar magnets with somewhat weaker properties.

The text explains that unlike electric charges, isolated magnetic north and south poles known as magnetic monopoles do not exist.

The text explains that (v) It is possible to make magnets out of iron and its alloys.

The text explains that for comparison refer to the Chapter 1, Figure 1.14(d).

The text explains that unlike electrostatics the field lines in magnetism do not indicate the direction of the force on a (moving) charge.

The text explains that physics 138 (ii) The tangent to the field line at a given point represents the direction of the net magnetic field B at that point.

The text explains that (iv) The magnetic field lines do not intersect, for if they did, the direction of the magnetic field would not be unique at the point of intersection.

The text explains that this gives us an idea of the magnetic field direction at various points in space.

The text explains that 5.2.2 Bar magnet as an equivalent solenoid In the previous chapter, we have explained how a current loop acts as a magnetic dipole (Section 4.9).

The text explains that we mentioned Ampere’s hypothesis that all magnetic phenomena can be explained in terms of circulating currents.

The text explains that the magnetic potential energy Um is given by U d m = ∫ τ θ θ ( ) = = − ∫mB d mB sin cos θ θ θ = –m.B (5.3) We have emphasised in Chapter 2 that the zero of potential energy can be fixed at one’s convenience.

The text explains that true only if the source of the field has a net non-zero magnetic moment.

The text explains that (5.1), (5.2) and (5.3) with the corresponding equations for electric dipole (Chapter 1), suggests that magnetic field at large distances due to a bar magnet of magnetic moment m can be obtained from the equation for electric field due to an electric dipole of dipole moment p, by making the following replacements: → E B , → p m , 0 0 1 4 4 μ ε → π π In particular, we can write down the equatorial field (BE) of a bar magnet at a distance r, for r >> l, where l is the size of the magnet: 0 3 4 E r μ = − π m B (5.4) Likewise, the axial field (BA) of a bar magnet for r >> l is: 0 3 2 4 A r μ = π m B (5.5)

The text explains that the arrow shows the direction of its magnetic moment.

The text explains that (a) In which configuration the system is not in equilibrium?

The text explains that (b) In which configuration is the system in (i) stable, and (ii) unstable equilibrium?

The text explains that thus, (a) PQ1 and PQ2 (b) (i) PQ3, PQ6 (stable); (ii) PQ5, PQ4 (unstable) (c) PQ6 5.3 MAGNETISM AND GAUSS’S LAW In Chapter 1, we studied Gauss’s law for electrostatics.

The text explains that there are no sources or sinks of B; the simplest magnetic element is a dipole or a current loop.

The text explains that all magnetic phenomena can be explained in terms of an arrangement of dipoles and/or current loops.

The text explains that the correct magnetic field lines are circling the straight conductor, as described in Chapter 4.

The text explains that magnetic field lines (like electric field lines) can never cross each other, because otherwise the direction of field at the point of intersection is ambiguous.

The text explains that note carefully the direction of field lines inside.

The text explains that example 5.4 (a) Magnetic field lines show the direction (at every point) along which a small magnetised needle aligns (at the point).

The text explains that can a system have magnetic moments even though its net charge is zero?

The text explains that if monopoles existed, the right hand side would be equal to the monopole (magnetic charge) qm enclosed by S. [Analogous to Gauss’s law of electrostatics, B s .∆ = ∫ μ0qm S where qm is the (monopole) magnetic charge enclosed by S .] (c) No.

The text explains that the average of the charge in the system may be zero.

The text explains that 5.4 MAGNETISATION AND MAGNETIC INTENSITY The earth abounds with a bewildering variety of elements and compounds.

The text explains that in the present section, we define and explain certain terms which will help us to carry out this exercise.

The text explains that the magnetic field in the interior of the solenoid was shown to be given by B0 = μ0 nI (5.8) If the interior of the solenoid is filled with a material with non-zero magnetisation, the field inside the solenoid will be greater than B0.

The text explains that the net B field in the interior of the solenoid may be expressed as B = B0 + Bm (5.9)

The text explains that physics 146 EXAMPLE 5.5 where Bm is the field contributed by the material core.

The text explains that in the latter case M and H are opposite in direction.

The text explains that example 5.5 A solenoid has a core of a material with relative permeability 400.

The text explains that explain the sense in which the solenoid acts like a bar magnet.

The text explains that 5.4 If the solenoid in Exercise 5.5 is free to turn about the vertical direction and a uniform horizontal magnetic field of 0.25 T is applied, what is the magnitude of torque on the solenoid when its axis makes an angle of 30° with the direction of applied field?

The text explains that 5.5 A bar magnet of magnetic moment 1.5 J T –1 lies aligned with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.22 T.

The text explains that (a) What is the amount of work required by an external torque to turn the magnet so as to align its magnetic moment: (i) normal to the field direction, (ii) opposite to the field direction?

The text explains that (b) What is the torque on the magnet in cases (i) and (ii)?

The text explains that 5.6 A closely wound solenoid of 2000 turns and area of cross-section 1.6 × 10 –4 m2, carrying a current of 4.0 A, is suspended through its centre allowing it to turn in a horizontal plane.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Chapter 5: Magnetism and Matter

prelims_traps: Do not treat 5.1 INTRODUCTION and MAGNETISM AND as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Magnetism and Matter; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Magnetism and Matter through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 5.1 INTRODUCTION and MAGNETISM AND to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
